data.title: NRO: Struggling To Grow In High Interest Rate Environment
data.description:
Overview

Neuberger Berman Real Estate Securities Income Fund (NYSE:NRO) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of diverse real estate investments. As we remain in a higher interest rate environment, there are plenty of high quality REITs that remain at a discounted valuation. However, it can be difficult to accumulate a position in multiple opportunities at the same time. So I've been looking for a solid real estate focused fund that maintains exposure to high quality holdings and is able to provide a mix of both income and capital appreciation.

Looking at the performance of NRO over the last twelve months, the price has decreased by about 6.3%. However, most of that decline happened over the last two week period as a result of the market volatility driven by President Trump's ongoing tariff proposals. When including all distributions that have been paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at about 3.2% over the same time frame. NRO has a generous starting dividend yield of 12.5%, which makes it an attractive choice for investors looking to create a sizeable supplemental income stream within their portfolio. However, I worry about the sustainability of the distribution going forward.

NRO has a public inception dating back to 2003, meaning we have over two decades worth of performance data to reference. The most updated reporting seems to indicate that the fund has trouble displaying any meaningful NAV growth. While growth has trended in a positive direction over the last fiscal year, the interest rate environment and market volatility still make it difficult for the fund to succeed in the near future. Additionally, it seems like NRO has had trouble generating a sufficient amount of earnings to fully support the annual distributions. So let's start by taking a look at NRO's underlying strategy and investment approach.

Fund Strategy

According to the most updated fact sheet, NRO has total assets of $224M that is spread across approximately 81 different holdings. The fund uses a blended approach by having a nearly 67% exposure to common stocks and an approximate 33% exposure to preferred stocks. From a sector perspective, Health Care investments account for the largest portion of NRO's portfolio at 12.1%. This is followed by exposure to Telecommunications and Data Center REITs, accounting for 11.6% and 10.6% respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up about 34.3% of the total portfolio so we can assume that these sectors will have the greatest impact on NRO's daily price change and total return potential.

I do like the Data center exposure that NRO provides, considering the projected growth to take place throughout the AI sector. As the demand and utility around Artificial Intelligence grows, so will the necessary spending on Data centers. For instance, McKinsey has a midrange estimate that sees the market share of Data centers increasing at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 22% through 2030. As we can see, NRO's top position is currently Equinix (EQIX) at 8.3% of assets. EQIX currently has a strong buy rating by Wall Street, with an average price target of $1,018 per share which indicates a potential upside of 31%.

NRO also has exposure to other high quality real estate investments like Prologis (PLD) and American Tower Corp. (AMT), accounting for 8.1% and 6.9% of assets respectively. I do like the mixed focus here as NRO maintains a diverse exposure to different industries, which can let the fund partake in sector specific bull markets. Here are some notable achievements of a few of the top holdings:

  • Welltower (WELL): saw its share price increase by over 170% over the last five years. WELL is set to capitalize on the growing demand for senior housing.
  • Sun Communities (SUI): has very conservative dividend payout ratio of approximately 58%.
  • Public Storage (PSA): has a higher dividend yield of 4.3% and has been able to increase its dividend at a CAGR of 8.45% over the last five years.

As part of the fund's strategy, NRO utilizes some leverage in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. Leverage currently amounts to 23.7% of assets, which I would like to see reduced in this higher interest rate environment. Higher interest rates directly translate to a higher cost of borrowing, which can make it more difficult for NRO to grow its earnings. Investors must also consider the risk that leverage could be used on an investment that doesn't work out as planned, which will amplify losses.

Valuation

Since NRO operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, the price now trades at a slight discount to NAV of about 5.4%. For reference, NRO has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.4% over the last three years, so I would consider the price to actually be trading around its fair value. Looking back over the last ten years below, we can see that NRO's price to NAV relationship is constantly shifting and I believe this can be attributed to the fund's ability to generate earnings.

The most recent annual report is dated as of October 2024 and reveals a few key things. Net investment income landed at $0.12 per share while net realized gains amounted to $1.16 per share for 2024. This resulted in a total growth from investment operations of $1.28 per share, which was a strong year of growth for NRO. However, the fund has failed to deliver meaningful NAV growth over the last five years due to the fund's reliance on net realized gains.

Net investment income has never exceeded $0.13 per share, which is a bit odd for a fund full of income producing REITs. It's also weird considering that a sizeable portion of the portfolio is allocated towards preferred securities that are more likely to pay out consistent income. As a result, NRO's growth is dependent on positive market conditions that can lead to net realized gains. In the future, I would love to see NRO's underlying performance improve by growing net investment income. This would create a more stable price and make it more likely for NRO to successfully navigate challenging macro conditions. For instance, here are the NAV amounts at each year end.

  • 2020: $3.89
  • 2021: $5.25
  • 2022: $3.40
  • 2023: $2.99
  • 2024: $3.90

To NRO's credit, the real estate sector has continued to suffer through headwinds ever since Covid happened in 2020. Covid drove markets down and then the Fed rapidly hiked interest rates shortly after, which caused the sector to decline. We haven't really been able to navigate through a 'normal' market over the last few years and real estate hasn't really had the chance to normalize. Hopefully the Fed will implement interest rate cuts, which should serve as a catalyst for growth in the real estate sector.

Risk Profile

The biggest risk will continue to be the current interest rate environment. Interest rates were aggressively hiked throughout 2022 and 2023 in response to record breaking inflationary data. Ever since then, the price has remained below its prior highs. A higher interest rate directly correlates to a higher cost of borrowing, as well as increased interest expenses that the fund has to pay out on its leverage. Since the real estate sector is heavily reliant on access to affordable debt capital, growth tends to slow in a higher interest rate environment. Since it becomes more costly to pursue growth initiatives like acquisitions, new developments, or marketing campaigns, businesses typically shift to focus on capital efficiency instead.

Looking at the price chart below, we can see how NRO's price has shared an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate. When interests rates were cut to near zero levels following the pandemic, NRO's price rapidly increased over the year. Therefore, I anticipate that future interest rate cuts will serve as a catalyst for growth.

However, the risk remains that we are likely to stay in a higher interest rate environment over the next twelve months, which can keep NRO's price suppressed. Inflation rates remain higher than the Fed anticipates and they've shifted their outlook to a more hawkish stance. At the end of 2024, the Fed estimated four interest rate cuts to take place over 2025, but this has now been reduced to only one estimated interest rate cut. If interest rates were to increase, we've likely see NRO's price go lower.

Dividend

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0312 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.5%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.3744 per share. Looking back over the dividend history, we can see that the payouts have remained flat since 2021. NRO also has a history of decreases in the past, but I don't actually think that the dividend is too large. I think the decreases ultimately come from the fund's flawed strategy. The fund needs to improve the net investment income so that it can offset losses on the sale of positions. For instance, NRO only covered the distribution two out of the last five years. Here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: ($1.51)
  • 2021: $1.75
  • 2022: ($1.48)
  • 2023: ($0.04)
  • 2024: $1.28

The annual report indicates that the majority of the distributions are coming from a combination of net investment income and return of capital. This could indicate that the portions of the dividend will be classified as ordinary dividend income. Conversely, return of capital distributions aren't taxed directly. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced and taxes are deferred until the time of sale. Based on your specific circumstance, a position in NRO may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate NRO as a hold due to the inconsistent NAV growth and weak internal earnings. The latest annual report reveals that the fund's net investment income has historically been weak. Net investment losses have weighed down any NAV momentum. The distribution amount doesn't seem to be an issue but the macroeconomic conditions still remain unfavorable. Higher interest rates can suppress valuations and make it a lot harder for NRO to grow.

data.title: Regions Financial: Capital Is A Focus Ahead Of Q1
data.description:
Shares of Regions Financial (NYSE:RF) have been hard hit during the recent market sell-off and have now essentially erased all of their gains over the past year with shares roughly 30% off their highs. With the stock set to report Q1 earnings on Thursday April 17th, this is a critical opportunity for the company to stabilize sentiment. I had been cautious on RF for some time, but upgraded shares to a “hold” in December. In hindsight, I should have just remained a sell, given shares have lost about 26%. In this article, I will preview Q1 results and whether investors should take a position into the release.

Recent growth has been sluggish despite a favorable geography

Regions Financial is among the larger regional banks in the country, and it primarily operates in the Southeast or Sun Belt region of the United States. RF is committed to growing further, primarily by deepening its exposure to key markets rather than expanding outside of core markets. Given the relatively fragmented state of the industry, this makes sense as there remains plenty of opportunity for RF to grow and scale inside its key markets. In particular, it is focused on Texas and Florida as growth drivers.

Before previewing results, it is worth reviewing recent results. In Q4, Regions earned $0.56, bringing full year earnings to $1.93. For the quarter as well as the year, the story was one of really modest growth, despite these ambitions. As you can see below, its deposits were essentially flat from a year ago. This meets my criteria of “stable” deposits, but RF has not really been growing.

Over time, we do need to see a bank grow its deposits in order to increase its loan book, drive net interest income (NII) and increase its EPS. One other notable factor is that its cost of deposits rose 6bps year over year, even as the Fed funds rate was 100bps lower. This reflects the fact that it had faced some outflows in noninterest-bearing (NIB) deposits, which now appear to be stabilizing, as well as repriced maturing time deposits at higher yields.

Because of higher funding costs, its 355bp net interest margin (NIM) while up 1bp sequentially was down 5bps from a year earlier. In Q1, we should see some modest benefit from a full quarter of the December rate cut. Its interest-bearing deposits exited Q4 at a 2.07% cost, down from a 2.13% average in Q4.

Given weak deposit growth, RF had $96.4 billion of loans, down $600 million sequentially and 2% from a year earlier. In particular, business loans were lower, and the company is gradually reducing its commercial real estate exposure (CRE). As with most banks, RF is also seeing a weakening of credit quality with nonperforming loans (NPL) rising. Pre-pandemic, its NPL ratio averaged 1.07%, so there may be some more weakening here. Charge-offs were 49bps, at the high-end of its 40-50bp long-term target.

Lack of growth likely to limit Q1 and full year EPS

Looking ahead, analysts are currently expecting the company to earn $0.51 in Q1. The company has beaten estimates in just five of the past ten quarters. Generally speaking, I like to see companies beat estimates at least 70% of the time, and this history is consistent with my view of RF being a mixed operator. Now, there are two fewer calendar days in Q1. This should be a $0.03 headwind to earnings all else equal, so if RF performed exactly in line with Q4, we would see about $0.53 of EPS.

There are several other factors to consider. First on its average balance sheet, I expect to see flattish to slightly down deposits. Typically, municipalities begin drawing dawn deposits after receiving property tax revenue in Q4; partially offsetting this, consumer balances benefit from tax refunds. RF is expecting some deposit growth after Q1, but I remain cautious on its ability to grow deposits after a fairly difficult time doing so over the past two years. I will also be focused on if RF was able to reduce the cost of deposits further.--I expect just modest improvement

On the loan side, management had guided that the loan pipeline is trending up and it expected H2 2025 growth. I am very cautious on the potential for loan growth. With so much macro uncertainty and rates staying higher, I do not expect businesses to engage in as much discretionary cap-ex, which will likely reduce loan demand longer term and challenge full year guidance.

Credit losses are likely a post-Q1 risk

On credit quality, the question for the sector is how much they react to tariffs and potential economics downside. In its first quarter, Wells Fargo (WFC) took $932 million in provisions, similar to last year’ $938 million and down from $1.1 billion in Q4. This is the big bank that most resembles regionals. I expect RF and others to similarly take a wait and see approach.

We are more likely to see large reserve builds in Q2 or Q3 if the economy does indeed turn down rather than in Q1, I expect. RF has $1.73 billion of allowances. I would rather see this above $1.85 billion or 2x NPLs, and so I expect to see some gradual builds. In particular, RF has $15 billion of CRE. It has just 10% exposure in office vs 29% in apartments and 7% to homebuilders.

With Sun Belt real estate valuations struggling, I could see some reserve builds in its apartment exposure, though I expect minimal losses. Within office, it has $241 million of nonperforming loans but just $111 million in reserves. While its loans have a low 69% LTV, this is a low coverage ratio, and I expect reserves to rise in 2025.

On noninterest revenue, asset growth should boost wealth management and capital markets. However with market volatility, I expect to see capital markets activity weaken considerably and be a headwind in Q2-Q4. Similarly with rates staying elevated, mortgage income is likely to be constrained.

Low adjusted capital is the primary headwind

RF also has a $30.7 billion securities portfolio, which has $2 billion of unrealized losses. Like many banks, RF has purchased securities when yields were lower and is now carrying them at a loss. It has substantial fixed rate hedges at lower yields, as well. As these mature, we should see some NII improvement, but it will be a very slow process with limited 2025 uplift.

These losses are also important to consider when looking at its capital position. RF ended the year with a 10.8% common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio, which is quite healthy at first glance. However when we factor in unrealized losses, capital dips to 8.8%, below its target of 9.25-9.75%.

In Q1, I expect both metrics to be down 10-20bps, but in Q2 if rates rise further, we could see losses worsen. At the start of the year, RF expects its losses to decline to $1.6 billion this year, adding 34bps to capital. I now expect just half of this benefit to be realized in 2025. This means RF will take longer to bring capital to its targeted range. I expect very limited repurchases after $58 million of Q4 buybacks, though I view its 5.3% dividend as secure.

RF’s weak adjusted capital position is an increased concern in a worsening macro environment, as it has less room to manage losses. This is one reason I have preferred Key (KEY), which has taken actions to enhance its capital position and reduce the impact of unrealized losses.

In terms of guidance, I do see downside risk to its targeted loan growth, which in turn could weigh on NII guidance. I also see some upside risk to charge-offs. I do think RF will make just modest tweaks (ie 0-2%), given how much uncertainty remains.

Shares are not expensive, but upside catalysts appear unlikely

Back in December, I had seen $2.20-$2.35 in EPS in 2025. Given slower balance sheet growth and more credit losses, I now see earnings of $1.90-$2.25. I appreciate this is a wider range than 3 months ago, but there is substantially more uncertainty on the economic outlook. I would expect $0.51-$0.53 in Q1 earnings, for a modest beat. Shares are just about 10x earnings, which is not expensive. As such, I do not see a need to sell shares, especially as there is unlikely to be a serious miss in Q1, in my view.

That said, in this period of uncertainty, I believe investors are likely to place a premium on banks with strong capital positions. Downside economic tail risks have risen over the past month. In the central case, RF can retain earnings to return capital to better levels, but in a downside case, capital will constrain its balance sheet flexibility further. As such, I struggle to see an argument for multiple expansion with capital likely to be below target all year. With banks like Key also around 10x earnings, I think there are names with more upside potential in the regional sector, and I remain a hold on RF.

data.title: Genmab's Outlook Beyond Darzalex's Patent Cliff
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Solomon Oshabaheebwa as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Genmab (GMAB) presents a potentially rewarding long-term investment opportunity, though current uncertainties warrant a cautious approach. The primary concern is the pending patent expiration for Darzalex, Genmab's blockbuster drug that contributed 65% of FY24 revenue. In this analysis, I assessed how Genmab is positioned to weather this significant transition. I examined the revenue potential of Genmab’s other commercial products, its late-stage pipeline, anticipated product launches, and balance sheet strength. I then made revenue projections that model "best-case" and "worst-case" scenarios as Darzalex royalties decline. Despite the looming patent cliff, my findings suggest that Genmab's robust pipeline and strategic positioning make it a compelling low-risk, high-reward opportunity for patient investors.

Company Overview

Genmab develops customized antibodies that target markers on the surface of cancerous cells or other molecules associated with various illnesses. They make the antibodies through five proprietary technology platforms termed: DuoBody, HexaBody, DuoHexaBody, HexElect, and Antibody-Drug Conjugates (ADCs). They have successfully licensed their technology to several Biotech behemoths, including Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Novartis (NVS), and Amgen (AMGN), that incorporate the antibodies in various therapies. These partnerships yield milestone revenues tied to certain development goals, and royalties on sales.

Until recently, Genmab did not participate in commercialization instead focusing on technology development. It began to expand its own commercialization capabilities with TIVDAK for cervical cancer in 2021, sharing commercial responsibilities and profits with Pfizer in various markets. Starting 2025, Genmab will have sole responsibility outside the US and China. The company also co-commercializes EPKINLY/TEPKINLY with AbbVie, leading efforts in the US and Japan while AbbVie takes charge in Europe. The company aims to expand its wholly owned portfolio in the near term with new candidates Rina-S and Acasunlimab.

DARZALEX’s dominance presents a concentration risk.

In FY2024, Genmab reported revenues of $3 billion of which 65% was generated from DARZALEX royalties. This heavy dependence on a single product poses a significant vulnerability to future revenues as patent expiration approaches. As I will discuss later, the other products on the market are unlikely to outgrow Darzalex sales in the near term.

J&J collaboration on DARZALEX is expiring soon.

J&J and Genmab have an existing global commercialization agreement in which Genmab granted J&J an exclusive license to develop and sell products containing daratumumab (the active ingredient in DARZALEX) for the treatment of patients with certain indications of multiple myeloma [MM] and light-chain [AL] amyloidosis. In exchange, Genmab receives up to 20% royalties from the net sales of these products paid until the expiration of the last relevant Genmab patents. These patents, after accounting for extensions, begin to expire in 2029 (U.S.), 2030 (Japan) and 2031 (Europe). Because of the nature of the agreement, DARZALEX royalties are projected to decline to near 0 by 2032, the only question is whether Genmab can open up new revenue streams to make up for this loss.

HexaBody-CD38 was a failed succession plan.

Patent expiration is a common risk in the Biotech sector with the potential to dramatically erode a company's revenue and market value. Pharmaceutical companies are typically granted 20-year patents from the point of discovery, which includes research and development time. This effectively leaves most drugs with approximately 10 years of market exclusivity after which competitors can introduce generic versions, often at substantially lower prices, resulting in a steep decline in sales (the "patent cliff"). Pharmaceutical companies often navigate this cliff by developing improved versions of their existing drugs, and proactively transitioning patients to the new formulations before the original patent expires, thereby maintaining their competitive edge and market position.

HexaBody-CD38 (GEN3014) was GenMab’s strategy to replace DARZALEX and maintain revenue streams from the same patient population. HexaBody-CD38 targets the same antibody CD38 as DARZALEX but is designed to be more potent. It uses Genmab’s HexaBody technology to form clusters upon target binding that make it better at mobilizing the complementary immune system to kill myeloma cells with higher potency. In head-to-head clinical trials against DARZALEX, Hexabody-CD38 showed promising results with a better overall response rate (55% versus 52%). But J&J did not find the results sufficient to warrant further investment. The decision made sense as the new drug must be much better than the old drug to convince patients and prescribers to abandon the old drug especially when the old drug gets cheaper with generic versions in the market. While the performance of HexaBody-CD38 demonstrated potential for Genmab’s HexaBody technology in future applications, it failed to succeed as a replacement for DARZALEX and left Genmab still exposed to the patent risk.

Can Existing Products Fill the DARZALEX Gap?

Genmab provided future sales projections for most of its commercialized products. These projections are largely in line with those made by other independent analysts but keep in mind that it shares revenue with collaborators on all its products. DARZALEX, Kesimpta, and TEPEZZA are expected to grow moderately at a high-single-digit compound annual growth rate [CAGR] to about $16.4 billion in 2030. TECVAYLI, TALVEY, RYBREVANT, have a much higher runway with projections assuming double-digit CAGRs but Genmab’s royalties on these products will remain much smaller than DARZALEX.

In TIVDAK (cervical cancer), starting in 2025, Pfizer will lead commercialization activities in the US and China with a 50:50 profit split, while Genmab will lead commercialization elsewhere and pay Pfizer royalties in the low teens. TIVDAK annual sales were $131M in 2024 and are projected to reach $863M in 2030.

For EPKINLY/TEPKINLY (large B-cell lymphomas), Genmab and AbbVie share profits 50:50 in the U.S. and Japan, while Genmab receives 22% to 26% royalties from other regions. Global sales of EPKINLY/TEPKINLY were $281M in 2024 and are expected to grow to $1.9B by 2029 with additional growth contingent on the outcomes of on-going phase 3 clinical trials.

Overall, Genmab has a large product portfolio with high expectations for sales growth. Even if these targets are met, DARZALEX royalties remain unmatched and the concentration risk will persist as the blockbuster drug will still account for around 70% of annual royalty revenue in 2030. Genmab's best hope is launching new products from its clinical pipeline to generate new revenue streams.

Diversified late-stage pipeline with potential product launches.

Genmab has 7 ongoing phase 3 trials, including 5 for EPKINLY (in collaboration with AbbVie), aiming to expand the patient population and indications in B-cell malignancies. Other programs involve wholly owned new therapies - Rina-S for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer, and Acasunlimab for solid tumors.

While clinical trials inherently possess a high risk of failure (some estimates suggest 50% – 60% failure rate), the risk is significantly lower for clinical trials expanding the use of an already approved drug primarily because the safety profile and pharmacokinetics of the drug are already established. I am therefore optimistic about the expected new product launches, especially for the expansion of EPKINLY, which is already FDA-approved.

The results of these clinical trials, if positive, will lead to new product launches between 2027 and 2031 with potential peak sales above $3 billion for EPKINLY, $2 billion from Rina S, and $1 billion from Acasunlimab. These products will certainly have a major impact on Genmab’s value and investors have good reason to keep an eye out for updates from the clinical studies to gain clarity on the probability of success.

Strength of Balance sheet

Genmab ended FY 2024 with a strong balance sheet with $2.9 billion in cash and investments and no interest-bearing long-term debt. Historically, Genmab converts about 35% of revenue to free cash flow, assuming this trend continues in the near term, the company will generate over $1 billion per year in free cash (even at the lower end of expected revenues as discussed in the next section). This cash position indicates that they can fully fund their clinical pipeline while maintaining substantial reserves for funding strategic acquisitions similar to the $1.8 billion acquisition of ProfoundBio, or return value to shareholders through share repurchases as they’ve done in the past.

Future revenue projections

The major risk for Genmab is Scientific Risk. I modeled two scenarios to assess its impact on Genmab's future revenue. In both scenarios, I assumed that DARZALEX sales will grow at a 7% CAGR until 2029, and then decline to $0 by 2032. Meanwhile, other commercialized products grow at 23% CAGR over the next five years, followed by a 10% growth rate thereafter.

In the first scenario, I assumed that all phase 3 trials will fail. Based on these assumptions, my model predicts that Genmab's revenue could reach its peak in 2029 and then bottom out at approximately $2.9 billion in 2032.

In the second scenario, I assumed that all phase 3 trials will succeed, with new products launching as expected from 2027 to 2031 and steadily growing to reach expected maturity revenues within 5 years after launch. In this scenario, the model predicts rapidly growing revenues between 2027 – 2029 as new products launch and then moderate growth thereafter as new product revenue offsets the loss of DARZALEX royalties, reaching ~$7.5 billion by 2033.

Valuation

The biotech sector has had low investor confidence since 2022 with several Biotech companies now trading at historically low sales multiples including Genmab’s current 3.9x. If we assume that this sales multiple remains flat, Genmab could be trading at a market cap of $12.5 billion in scenario 1 and $30 billion in scenario 2. Thus in a worst-case scenario, the price remains roughly flat, while in a best-case scenario, the price goes up nearly 3x the current value.

Risks

There are some risks worthy of note. Rina S will directly compete with Elahere (mirvetuximab soravtansine-gynx) by Immunogen (owned by ABBV), which is also an ADC targeting platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. Elahare received FDA approval in 2024 giving it a head start in this space. EPKINLY also faces competition from several CAR-T therapies and other antibody therapies that are already approved for the same indications in both B-cell malignancies and follicular lymphoma. The competition from these alternative therapies could significantly decrease market penetration for Genmab’s candidates resulting in underwhelming sales.

Additionally, the company intends to make more acquisitions which could increase the debt burden and lower future cash inflows. Revenues from the other commercialized products could also decelerate under pressure from competitors.

Conclusion

On balance, I believe Genmab’s current market cap of ~$11.8 billion roughly aligns with a scenario where all its phase-3 trials fail. The company has a Quant score of 3.37 and a “HOLD” rating which is highly leveraged by an “F” growth factor grade while the other factors remain strong. It is likely to receive an upgrade if the growth problem is solved with the introduction of new products with substantial contributions to revenue and high growth prospects. If the ongoing phase 3 trials are successful, Genmab could see a 3x appreciation in value. Also, consider that the company can utilize its strong cash position to open new avenues for growth such as new acquisitions or advancing other early-stage clinical programs.

At the current valuation, the downside is limited while the potential upside is substantial making it potentially a low-risk high-reward investment. The only reason I didn’t give it a buy rating is because I believe it will take some time for this value to materialize as major results from clinical trials are not expected until 2026. Investors should watch the company closely, particularly for updates from the clinical studies. If these studies prove successful, a price bump could be expected.

data.title: Estee Lauder: Do Not Expect Beauty Reimagined To Turnaround The Company In The Short-Term
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Gunn Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Estee Lauder (NYSE:EL) is one of the largest cosmetic companies in the world that is headquarted in New York, United States and employs about 62k full-time employees. The company's products include skin care, fragrance, makeup, and hair care; these products are sold in more than 150 countries under multiple renowned brands such as Estee Lauder, Jo Malone, Clinique, Aramis, and more.

Based on my analysis, although EL's share price has declined about 85% from its 2022 high, it is not a buying opportunity. While the company launched Beauty Reimagined, macroeconomic factors such as deteriorating consumer sentiment and the current trade war will continue to weigh on the performance of EL. In this report, I will demonstrate why investors should exercise caution and avoid EL in their portfolios.

Latest Developments

For 2Q25, EL generated $4 billion in revenues, representing a y/y decline of 6.45%. Revenue deterioration is primarily due to weaker sales across all product categories and region. Specifically, revenue from skin care products declined by 12% due to a more challenging retail environment in Asia Pacific while revenue from hair care products fell by 8% due to a continued lack of demand in the company's salon channel. From a geographical perspective, sales in EMEA and Asia Pacific saw the largest deterioration with a revenue decline of 6% and 11% respectively.

Operating margin for the period has deteriorated to 11.54% from 13.60% in the same period last year due to higher SG&A. Net income continues to remain in the negative territory; In 2Q25, net income margin deteriorated to -14.74% from 7.31% in the same period last year.

Looking ahead, EL anticipates financial deterioration to persist. Overall, organic sales is expected to decline by 8% to 10%. Furthermore, currency translation is likely to weigh on net sales by about 2%. Additionally, due to a change in geographical mix in earnings, effective tax will surge to 36% from 30.50%.

Challenging Consumer Environment Will Likely Exacerbate EL's Financial Deterioration

Currently, we are seeing multiple indicators that suggests that consumer sentiment across the world will remain weak. Based on Upside's consumer spending report, 58% of consumers will reduce spending due to a poor perception of the economy. Moreover, in a special survey conducted by Conference Board, consumers are still extremely price sensitive.

Thus far, EL's revenue deterioration is primarily due to a weak consumer sentiment in Asia Pacific and EMEA; the Americas region has largely outperformed. However, latest macroeconomic data suggests that EL will start to see further sales deterioration in the Americas region. Based on the latest data from the University of Michigan's US consumer survey, consumer sentiment has plunged 34.2% y/y to 50.8 in April 2025 from 77.2 in the same period last year; the latest survey highlighted that the risk of recession has increased and expectations for "business conditions, personal finances, incomes, inflation, and labor markets" have weakened substantially.

Tariff Tsunami and Reciprocal Tariffs will Weigh on Beauty Reimagined and Expanded Profit Recovery and Growth Plan

In an effort to combat the company's financial deterioration, EL has introduced and launched Beauty Reimagined to restore revenue growth and achieve double-digit operating margin within the next few years. Through Beauty Reimagined, EL will: (1) rapidly expand its portfolio presence, (2) boost consumer-facing investments such as increasing advertising spending, (3) expand its profit recovery and growth plan, and (4) optimize its operations by re-evaluating key supplier relationships and improving its supply chain network.

It is important to note that EL is engaged in manufacturing and distribution on the global scale. Even though EL may attempt to optimize its operations through workforce reductions, there is a strong likelihood that these efforts will be offset directly or indirectly by President Trump's tariff tsunami and reciprocal tariffs by other countries. Global companies like EL may attempt to localize its manufacturing and distribution; however, it will take some time before these companies can properly insulate themselves.

Moreover, the current trade war will exacerbate and accelerate the shift in consumer behaviors that we are seeing in recent years. Due to the rise in nationalism, Chinese consumers are already leaning more closely to local brands over international brands. Moreover, EL is already facing deteriorating sales in Asia Pacific; if the trade war persists, there is a strong likelihood that the company's sales will continue to weaken; in such a case, no amount of cost-saving initiatives will help the company.

Investors Should Expect Further Decline in Cash Reserves and Dividends Reduction In the Near Future

As Beauty Reimagined only recently launched, and we are still in the early stages of a global trade war, it is unlikely that we will see EL recovering in the near future. Hence, there is a likelihood that EL will burn through its cash reserves as it tries to pivot and navigate through the current challenges. Currently, EL only has $2.58 billion in cash and cash equivalents. Moreover, in the past twelve months, EL has net change in cash of -$1.35 billion; if this keeps up, there is a strong likelihood that EL will need to tap on the debt market to strengthen its liquidity.

As such, investors should not expect the current dividend distribution rate to be maintained. There is a strong possibility that EL will continue to reduce its dividends in an effort to preserve liquidity. In fact, in the past two quarters, EL already reduced its dividends by about 46% from $0.66 per share to $0.35 per share.

EL's 80% Drawdown From Its 2022 High Does Not Make the Company's Valuation Attractive

Ostensibly, it may appear that it is a good time to accumulate EL as the company's share price has declined about 85% from its 2022 high. However, from a relative valuation point of view, the company is still considered expensive. On a forward P/E non-GAAP basis, EL is trading at 39.46x as compared to its sector median of 15.72x. Even if we account for the company's debt, the company is trading at a forward EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA of 1.84x and 14.16x as compared to the sector median of 1.56x and 9.92x.

Hence, I would like to emphasize and reiterate, investors should exercise caution at this stage and should not be fooled into catching a falling knife. There are better avenues and opportunities out there in the market where investors can put their money to work.

Closing Remarks

Overall, although EL launched Beauty Reimagined as an effort to turnaround the company, investors should expect continued underperformance in the short term. The current global trade environment coupled with shifts in consumer behaviors and expectations will continue to weigh on the performance of EL. Moreover, the company's current valuation is not a screaming buy; investors will do better if they continue to exercise patience and put their money to work elsewhere.

EL is set to release its 3Q25 results on 1st May 2025. Previously, as many of the current tariffs were not yet imposed, the implications on EL were not discussed in the earnings call. Hence, in the upcoming earnings call, there is a strong possibility that management and analysts will center their discussion around the implications of the tariffs. Investors should pay close attention to how EL's management intend to navigate through the upcoming uncertainty.

data.title: Amazon: What ROIC Of AWS Says About The Stock's Perceived Fair Value
data.description:
Amazon, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) has been hit as hard as any of its peers in the famed Magnificent Seven (Mag 7) group of elite tech, retail, and communications stocks that account for nearly thirty percent of the S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

And yet, AMZN remains among the most attractive of these mega-caps, and the recent YTD decline of nearly 16 percent has only made it even more compelling. Not quite at fair value just yet, as I hope to show, but we seem to be getting there.

At a price to forward sales ratio close to its 5Y average, AMZN looks like it is at or very close to fair value. I say this in spite of it P/S multiple being well above the sector median because, for all practical purposes, AWS is still the one segment supporting earnings. Implicit in that is the need to look at this as an investment in cloud, not in retail and not yet in advertising.

I do not claim that other segments are not profitable, but my purpose with this article is to show the extent of AWS's ability to offset any kind of weakness in its retail and other operations, and act as a support to Amazon's stock price.

Allow me to begin with a quick look at a few key metrics that will set the base for my thesis.

How AWS Changed the Fortunes of Amazon.com, Inc.

I believe it would be more than a little pedantic to go through AWS' entire history with this audience, so we shall restrict our analysis to the past five years. My intention, as I alluded to earlier, is to showcase how this business segment, which Jeff Bezos called "A radical idea" in the FY14 Annual Report, is now the mainstay of Amazon's massive ship.

Driving Return on Capital

In that same Annual Report, Bezos made a statement that proved prophetic:

I’m optimistic that AWS will have strong returns on capital. This is one we as a team examine because AWS is capital intensive. The good news is we like what we see when we do these analyses. Structurally, AWS is far less capital intensive than the mode it’s replacing – do-it-yourself datacenters – which have low utilization rates, almost always below 20%. Pooling of workloads across customers gives AWS much higher utilization rates, and correspondingly higher capital efficiency. Further, once again our leadership position helps: scale economies can provide us a relative advantage on capital efficiency. We’ll continue to watch and shape the business for good returns on capital.

A maddeningly simple concept that has now grown to $107.6 billion in sales as of FY24, but what about the "strong returns on capital" that Bezos predicted?

We shall restrict ourselves to invested capital so we build a better picture of returns on active investments. When seen through that lens, it becomes self-evident that this core profitability metric has a high correlation to AMZN share price growth. When ROIC began dropping in FY22, share price followed. Of course, ROIC is tied to net profitability, so when net income turned negative that year, the annual return on invested capital turned negative as well.

It took an entire year for the net income and ROIC to recover, forcing the stock to stay below the $150 mark through almost the entire period. Our interest here is in AWS' contribution to the bottom line during that two-year window. To that end, I present the segmental annual results for FY22.

AWS had contributed 16 percent to overall revenue. Not exceptional in and of itself, but the $81 billion that it brought in that year accounted for ALL operating profitability. All $12.3 billion of it, and completely offsetting operating losses in the two retail segments.

When that positive EBIT hit the bottom line, it was negative, primarily due to other operating expenses. Net income came in at -$2.7 billion. Net invested capital using the operating approach (net working capital + net PP&E + acquired intangibles + goodwill + other) works out to a little over $240 billion. Further validating this with the financing approach to calculating invested capital (total debt + deferred taxes + other liabilities + preferred stock + common equity), we get a figure of $234 billion. Averaging those to $237 billion, we get an ROIC of approximately -1.14 percent.

My claim is that without AWS, Amazon's ROIC would have been significantly deeper in the red. In that regard, it acted as a floor for AMZN's stock price.

Advancing this thesis to FY24, it is once again self-evident that AWS has contributed the bulk of Amazon's profitability. While contributing only 17 percent to the top line, AWS reported an EBIT of $39.8 billion, contributing nearly 60 percent to the consolidated EBIT figure of $68.59 billion.

What to Expect in FY25

Now for the prickly subject of tariffs. CEO Jassy has recently indicated that Amazon's retail customers are likely to see price increases from sellers who "will pass that cost on". His reassurance that the company is "doing everything we can to try and keep prices the way they’ve been for customers — as low as possible." could mean that the company will bear some of the additional cost so the price jumps for customers are kept within bounds. Furthermore, demand is likely to drop for items that are subject to the highest tariff slabs, which means there will likely be more than a little weakness at the bottom line.

This is precisely where AWS and its contribution to ROIC will keep the stock trading at levels that would otherwise be unsustainable through FY25. I would remind investors that AWS is not completely immune to tariffs just because it offers digital services; the hit will come from higher technology costs related to the hardware it acquires to keep growing its operations.

One opportunity for AMZN is to put a tight cap on any capital expenditures not related to either maintenance or its retail business. By restricting invested capital in this fashion, the contribution to ROIC from AWS will be even more pronounced. This is one way to ensure a high ROIC in what I foresee to be a tough year for the company. The CEO's assurances should be taken with a pinch of salt, as they say, because the retail business has high exposure to China and other nations that the tariffs are targeting.

Whatever hit we see to Amazon's retail topline at home and overseas could be partially ameliorated by front-loading inventory, or as Jassy referred to it, "strategic forward inventory buys", which means we may not see its effect until at least 3Q25 or later. When it does hit, I suspect the impact will not be minimal. This eventuality, fortunately, will see AWS come to the rescue with its strong bottom-line profitability. As long as AMZN keeps its non-AWS capex to a bare minimum, the stock should be able to weather up to $40 billion or more in operating losses in the retail segment and still show positive ROIC. Assuming AWS revenues keep growing over FY25, that buffer could get bigger over time.

What Are My Risk Considerations?

My recommendation, as I initiate coverage on AMZN, is a Hold. I do have faith that AWS will play a major role in offering support for the stock through FY25, but I am equally aware of the risks. The most likely one is an inordinate and sustained dip in retail revenue growth.

Now that one sees clearly that revenue growth could take a heavy hit from tariffs, one should look at AWS' ROIC as the primary indicator of intrinsic value. This is where support for the stock will come from, as I see it. We shall attempt to swoop in on this sweet spot from an ROIC perspective.

A 20 percent return on invested capital in the last full-fiscal against a five-year average of 14 percent should have allowed the stock to move up further from where it was at the start of the year. Notwithstanding the general market disruption that began shortly after Trump took office, there is also another loose end, which is that the stock price has never been rewarded to this degree even when ROIC was approaching 24 percent in FY21. $175 seems to be the sweet spot for this stock even at those ROIC levels.

A closer look at the price chart above (purple line) suggests that any breach of the $175 level results in a rapid pullback to below that level. The YTD decline has yet to bring it down again, but I think it likely will.

On the whole, if this has indeed been the result of the market being unwilling to keep the stock above $175 despite record-breaking ROIC, then a further haircut on that $175 would be well-justified in this time of even greater uncertainty at the global level.

I shall not attempt to quantify that downside from $175 because the hit to the retail segment is unknowable at this point. We should be getting some fresh updates when 1Q25 results are out later this month, but the retail segment's 40 percent contribution to ROIC should last until 2Q25, by my estimation. Depending on how much of an inventory build up we see across 1Q and 2Q (the "strategic forward inventory buys" that Jassy referred to), we should be able to gauge the runway for further retail growth and assess how long it might be until the full effect of tariffs is brought to bear on Amazon's retail operations.

Until I have that visibility, I cannot recommend anything beyond a Hold. At the current price of $185, I think the stock is slightly overpriced compared to where it traded at peak ROIC - around $175. The stock's fortunes are now tied in squarely with the ROIC of AWS, so the only way I see the price going up from here is if Amazon's management is willing to make those capital investment sacrifices in its retail business and favor more efficient spending on maintaining and growing AWS infrastructure and offerings.

One particular point that the CEO highlighted on the 4Q24 call gives me confidence that these capex adjustments are, in fact, being made (bolded for emphasis by author):

Chips are the key ingredient in the compute that drives training and inference. Most AI compute has been driven by NVIDIA chips and we obviously have a deep partnership with NVIDIA and will for as long as we can see into the future.

However, there aren't that many generative AI applications at large scale yet. And when you get there as we have with apps like Alexa and Rufus, cost can get steep quickly. Customers want better price performance and it's why we built our own custom AI silicon.

By bringing down retail operating costs, AMZN can filter more dollars down to the bottom line. Combined with higher but more return-friendly capital spending on the AWS side of things, it should make for a very compelling ROIC profile despite the headwind risks I have discussed.

As I take leave of you, I would advise acquiring shares below $175 and then using a dollar cost averaging method to either optimize your cost basis if the stock goes up or reduce it if the stock declines further. I do not presume to know what the market is thinking right now, but the word 'overvaluation' is very likely buzzing around every investor's mind. I urge you to play it safe and leave yourself with an adequate margin of safety down from the market's perceived ROIC-based fair value of $175. Hold for now.

data.title: Trump Weighs New Tariffs And Auto Sector Relief
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Uncertainty lingers

Market participants remain wary of President Donald Trump's trade policies, with latest updates indicating more tariffs - and rollbacks - may be on the way. Pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports are under scrutiny for potential tariffs, while the automobile sector may be offered some respite in the days ahead.

Review underway: On April 1, the Trump administration began investigating the impact of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports on national security, likely setting the stage for tariffs on the sectors. The Department of Commerce's pharma investigation covers generic and non-generic drugs, active pharma ingredients, and derivative products. The semiconductor probe covers items including leading-edge chips, chipmaking equipment, silicon wafers, and "downstream products that contain semiconductors."

The probes will look into current and projected demand for the pharma and semiconductor products, the ability of domestic production to meet demand, and potential for export restrictions. The chip probe will also assess "the ability of foreign nations to weaponize their control over semiconductors and SME supply chains." The investigations were initiated under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which allows the president to adjust imports that are found to threaten national security.

Tariff relief? In other news, Trump floated a potential reprieve for the 25% tariffs on automobile and auto part imports, given the industry's deeply-integrated North American supply chains. "I'm looking at something to help some car companies with it," Trump told reporters. "They're switching to parts that were made in Canada, Mexico and other places… they need a little bit of time, because they're going to make them here." Trump's signal was enough to lift top automakers' shares - GM, F, STLA - which ended on a higher note on Monday.

What else is happening...

WSB survey results: Better trade deals for the U.S. are expected.

Yellen: Bond selloff signals 'loss of confidence' in policymaking.

Consumer polls - Mixed inflation outlook, confidence worsens.

Trump administration to end rule capping credit card late fee.

data.title: PepsiCo's Valuation Hasn't Been This Low In A Decade - Time To Buy
data.description:
If the stock market doesn't like one thing, it's uncertainty.

The US-induced trade tensions, with back-and-forth announcements on the shape or size of import tariffs, have wreaked havoc on the market, with companies unable to make long-term CAPEX commitments as the rules of the game change daily.

Without clarity, it's particularly difficult for stock-pickers to be opportunistic, even in falling markets. At the moment, stocks are driven not by fundamentals but rather by politics.

In times like these, I prefer to flee to safety. I buy consumer staples such as my favorite, PepsiCo, Inc. (NASDAQ:PEP), reducing the risk in my portfolio while collecting a 3.75% dividend.

I have owned PepsiCo's shares in my portfolio for years now, tactically trimming or adding exposure based on its valuations. As the stock has fallen from its 52-week high of $183/share to the current $144, the environment is ripe for me to provide an update to my last coverage from the end of 2023.

Since my last coverage, the stock has underperformed the broader market (SP500), as income-oriented investors fled towards less risky, yet attractive, fixed-income and concerns grew over the long-term impact of weight-loss drugs from Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lily (LLY), raising worries about demand impact.

Likewise, from a health standpoint, both beverage giants PepsiCo and Coca-Cola (KO) are facing scrutiny from the government due to their potential health implications and market practices, such as price discrimination.

I call this the perfect storm for the company and a potential opportunity for dividend growth investors and investors looking for safety, as PepsiCo's shares are trading at a rare discount.

PepsiCo's Challenges

If you were to choose between owning Coca-Cola's business or PepsiCo's, which one would you choose?

No doubt KO is the undisputed leader in the beverage industry, but PepsiCo's portfolio offers diversified revenue streams from beverages, snack, and convenience foods brands, better withstanding market shocks and product-specific challenges while offering more stable growth.

My pick is PepsiCo.

PepsiCo's EPS growth over the past 15 years has been relatively shallow, 5.7% annually, as a result of a poor operational performance between 2012-2016, with management turning the ship around in 2021-2023, improving its bottom line with growth reaching 8-13% EPS annually.

In 2024, the EPS grew by 7%, helped by markets like Latin America and Europe, with weakness in the US with inflation-wary customers.

Yes, 7% growth is well below the 12% growth achieved the year prior, but 2024 was a year full of challenges with stretched customers and the safety recall in Quaker Foods unit.

On the last earnings call, management guided for relatively mild growth in 2025, expecting "mid-single-digit" EPS growth, similar to analyst's expectations of ~4.7% growth.

Relatively weak growth in 2025 is a direct result of ongoing price increases tailored to offset the declining volume in North America in snacks and beverages, as weaker consumer spending is impacting the company's performance.

Raising prices amidst inflation-wary customers might seem like a counterintuitive strategy, but PepsiCo is in a position where it cannot afford to absorb the extra costs as its operating margin contracted, and 2025-2026 might prove to be challenging years, and the stock price action is a direct reflection of that.

However, if you are a long-term-oriented investor, the undervalued stock is a perfect candidate for one's portfolio. That's why I added more shares to my portfolio, around $150/share, and if the stock price drops towards $135-$140, I'll add more as PepsiCo's fundamentals remain safe and sound.

Rocky Outlook

PepsiCo's products are marketed worldwide, yet the business benefits from secular tailwinds in beverage subcategories like energy drinks and further expansion in markets such as Africa, Asia, and Latin America, which are still underpenetrated, particularly in its snack business.

The good news is that the appetite for beverages and snacks tends to remain stable during economic cycles. JPMorgan, for instance, has raised the odds of a US recession to 60%, up from 30% at the beginning of the year. If this negative forecast materializes, PepsiCo's business should be more resilient, but I would still expect sales and stock price weakness.

The US-induced trade war is likely to lead to higher costs and disruptions in the supply chains. Due to its size, PepsiCo's end-to-end supply chain could prove more resilient, helping to absorb shocks and allowing more effective cost negotiation for its raw inputs, such as sugar, oil, and seasoning purchases, responsible for a relatively minor margin squeeze despite the inflationary environment.

That's not to say that the industry doesn't have inherent challenges that need to be overcome to continue generating attractive returns.

We see customers shift towards more health-conscious alternatives. As 60% of PepsiCo's revenue is generated in North America via Frito-Lay and Pepsi beverages, with another 40% generated from Europe, Asia-Pacific, the Middle East, and Africa, the company is exposed to various regulations.

For instance, increased taxes on sugary products, high-sodium products, saturated fat, and plastic packages all pose challenges to its volume growth and pricing.

Then, we have the GLP-1 receptor agonist drug class that suppresses appetite, altering consumer behavior and potentially impacting the demand for fast foods, beverages, and snacks.

GLP-1 drugs are responsible for making its users feel fuller, leading to lower calorie intake, and while we don't yet have long-term data available on how consumer preference shifts, the reduced consumption of calorie-dense products could have implications on PepsiCo's core product lines.

Since I named this section "rocky outlook," certainly I should add here the risk of sticky inflation, being the byproduct of import tariffs, further impacting consumer spending, PEP's market share, and squeezing margins.

PepsiCo's risks shouldn't be taken lightly. Yet, the business remains a global powerhouse in beverages and snacks, supported by a well-recognized portfolio of consumer favorite brands with a loyal customer base and a $92B annual revenue run rate, confirming its successful manufacturing/supply chain/marketing execution.

As snacks contribute more to the business than beverages, around 54% of the company's revenue, I want to just highlight that back in 2024, PepsiCo controlled 23% of the market, ahead of competitors, and this is, in particular important, as it allows the company to retain industry-leading pricing power.

Let's not forget that PepsiCo has been a major partner to leading retailers such as Costco (COST) and Walmart (WMT) for decades, creating mutual value by PepsiCo better penetrating the market due to favorable treatment and the retailers gaining cost efficiency, something I call a "win-win relationship."

Shareholder Returns

PepsiCo has paid quarterly dividends since 1965, and it earns the company the revered title of Dividend King for increasing the dividend for over 50 years.

Due to the rare 27% drawback from its all-time high, PEP now offers investors an attractive 3.75% dividend yield.

To put this into perspective, PEP hasn't offered this high of a dividend yield in the last decade; only during the short-lived recession caused by COVID-19 did the yield spike to 3.68%.

I find the $1.355/share dividend very appealing. It's supported by a healthy 65% dividend payout ratio, and in the last five years, the dividend has grown at a 7.25% rate.

In fact, despite the headwinds, PEP announced a 5% dividend increase starting June 2025, sticking to its dividend growth policy.

Analysts are forecasting another increase coming next year, that should bring the total annual dividend to ~$6.04, which would mark a 6% increase.

Whether a 5-6% increase is in store for the next year is difficult to say at this stage, but even as the company navigates the challenges, I expect mid to high-single-digit dividend growth is likely until the end of the decade.

This provides a handsome balance between dividend yield and growth.

PepsiCo is also active when it comes to share buybacks, and its float has fallen by 7.1% in the last decade, with investors benefiting from the simple supply/demand dynamics.

Valuation

Overall, the stock market is trading at a premium to its historical valuations (despite the sell-off), and in pricey markets, bargain-hunting is virtually impossible as the tide lifts all boats.

Nonetheless, PEP's shares appear to be in a downward spiral, falling as much as 27% from its all-time high, currently trading at a Blended P/E of 17.6x.

To be fair, PEP's shares were expensive between 2019-2023, trading above the blue line below in a range of 22-28x its earnings.

From my standpoint, buying shares of slowly growing, defensive businesses at a P/E higher than 20, is a good way to earn sub-par ROR.

PEP's 15-year average valuation has been 22.2x its earnings, which gives us a good idea of the 21% discount in today's share price.

However, as I explained above, PEP's near-term outlook is rocky due to inflation, GLP-1 drugs, government scrutiny, and potential recession, all contributing to the stock price weakness.

I don't expect a reversal to its P/E of 22 in the near term, particularly as the forward growth will be pressured in 2025, as per FactSet:

  • 2025: EPS of $8.26E, YoY growth of 1%
  • 2026: EPS of $8.83E, YoY growth of 7%
  • 2027: EPS of $9.44E, YoY growth of 7%

I see a fair valuation right now, around 19-20x its earnings, with a potential for 12-13% total ROR (incl. dividends) over the following few years.

I find double-digit ROR attractive, with a quality holding like PepsiCo because as we face uncertainty in other areas of the market, drinking sodas and snacking on chips is set to stay with us, no matter the economic cycle.

Investor's Takeaway

All in all, PepsiCo's price weakness is fully justified due to sticky inflation, reduced consumer spending, government scrutiny, GLP-1 drugs, and the risk of recession.

However, an opportunity to buy a quality business like PepsiCo is rare, and I expect the negatives are priced in, and in case of recession, the business will likely offer above-average resilience due to its defensive beverage/snack portfolio.

Trading at a 10-year low of 17.6x its P/E, with a 3.75% dividend yield, I am increasing my exposure to PEP's business, looking to add more shares if the price contracts further towards $140/share.

data.title: Bechtle: From Overvaluation Into Fair Valuation
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

I've been covering European IT businesses for some time. Some with success, such as CapGemini (OTCPK:CGEMY) and SAP (SAP), others not so much, such as Atos SE (OTCPK:AEXAY).

While I am in no hurry to invest in the company that I am going to review for you today, I am saying it's a good company to have on your radar going forward because this company is likely to enjoy an upside on a forward basis. It may not be a growth monster - at least not today - but Bechtle AG (OTCPK:BECTY) has moved from a significant position of overvaluation to one of, at the very least, fair value - and this is something that is worth noting.

In this article, I will take you through the basics of the IT company Bechtle, a €6B revenue business that is as old as I am, founded about 40 years ago in Germany. The company trades natively as a component on the MDAX under the ticker BC8 and is headquartered in Neckarsulm, Germany.

Started by a few former students, a typical "garage" type of business, it has grown impressively to its current operations. Also, it's somewhat different in structure than both CapGemini, SAP, and Atos.

Let's see what we have going on under the hood here.

Bechtle - More of a holding than a very clear business

One of the characteristic parts of Bechtle is the fact that it actually owns shares in over 100 businesses across the world. The holding company, Bechtle, is, therefore, no more than a parent organization providing centralized services to subsidiaries. This includes IR services, corporate comms, HR, risk management, and Corporate IT.

However, its subsidiaries are not by name or, as you can see above, in other ways, separate entities. Instead, all subsidiaries usually start with the name Bechtle. Some of the ones included here are Bechtle Logistik & Service, Bechtle SystemHaus Holding, Bechtle Managed Services, etc.

The company's operations are found primarily in Germany, Switzerland, and Austria, meaning the German-speaking part of Europe, but it also has an international profile to its operations. Bechtle operations are split essentially into two businesses, the IT Systemhaus & managed services, which includes every single service business and specialized competence centers across Europe, and an E-commerce IT service with a portfolio of over 70,000 products sold using both traditional catalogue, something still very much "in" in Germany, as well as telesales and online sales.

See why I categorize the company as "interesting" due to this business and sales mix? I do not think you will find a lot businesses operating in this fashion.

Bechtle is an IT company that, unlike some others in Germany, focuses on what makes US-based IT companies appealing. That is a combination of growing earnings as well as growing dividends. Bechtle's dividend comes to 2.1% at this time, and this follows a massive crash since ZIRP. At its peak, Bechtle traded above €61/share for the native, it's now down to €33/share. The company has a market cap of around €4.2B, and, like many German companies, applies an extremely restrictive or conservative use of debt. Current long-term debt/cap is less than 23%.

The company has recorded growth even in difficult market environments and has averaged a rate of EPS growth above 11% per year over the past 20 years. This is well above the market and company average. Furthermore, the company is not a business that "misses" estimates. It hits them - and usually quite accurately, with either miss or beats over the past 10 years, even with a 10-20% margin of error.

I do not believe it inaccurate, therefore, to call Bechtle safe and capable and not prone to surprises. The share price might be - but this is a very different thing.

This lack of surprise for top and bottom line numbers can be seen in the 2024 report, which we have to look at.

The international segment is growing even more than the home market, considered by the company to be Germany. While the company reduced In EBIT for the year, as well as EBIT margin, this was related to cost increases and inflation, which did not spare Bechtle as a business either - much like the other companies on the market, both European and international. The company saw a rise in Gross Margin for the 2024 year YoY, but this was not enough to compensate against the costs seen here.

The company is practicing active cash management to increase cash flow, which has improved OCF from a low of €117M in 2022 to over €550M in 2024, improving liquidity and credit profile (though I would argue this is not even something the company struggles with).

Due to flat earnings levels, the company keeps its dividend flat as well - you'll want to expect the company to size the dividend according to its earnings, but given company communication, I don't expect easy "cuts" here to the payout, despite temporarily lower earnings. This is confirmed by the company's 2023-2025 trends.

The company is an organization that has been awarded a number of public contracts and work, the latest of which is work for Germany's Federal Ministry of Interior/community, and keeps winning EU OCRE tenders, the latest of which is cloud service and research/education in 13 countries.

What I would focus on are the company's basic financials. For 2024, the company generated €6.3B in revenue, upon which it made 18.1% GM. This is lower than our IT peers, but this is not just a service business. The companies then manage a 5-6% operating margin, again low for peers, but there are reasons for it. Given its low debt, the company's interest expenses are very low, and net income margins are at or close to 4%. The company has no SBC or dilution problems, it also doesn't really do buybacks as such. The company's dividend stands at a payout of less than 50% and is safe. Book value based on equity alone is over €15/share, and the company has a net debt of less than €100M (Source: TIKR.com Paywalled Link, Bechtle).

FCF for the company is excellent and has been growing very nicely.

And the margins here are above 7.3%. The company has no cash issues, with over €640M on hand as of the end of the year, which is well over 10% of its market cap. Shareholder yield is, in my view, a good way to value how the company rewards it shareholders and how it performs. Using the dividends paid and net debt paydowns (subtracting debt issued from debt paid back) and looking at market capitalization, we get a shareholder yield for the company of just south of 3%, which is very solid.

Comparing the company to what I view as its closest two peers yields decent results as well.

Fundamentals for the company are very solid, and I don't see any near-term danger or risk of fundamental deterioration. Forecasts are currently at a growth of 5-6% this year, followed by a reversal to double-digit growth rates in 2026E. Keep in mind that this company doesn't really miss any estimates.

Let me show you the valuation.

Valuation for Bechtle - Potentially attractive after a significant ZIRP-related drawdown.

Bechtle usually trades at 26-27x P/E. There is something to be said for the valuation premium being justified given the company's history, operating and gross margins, as well as the company's exposure to the public markets and governmental contracts in Europe. There's also the company's unfailing forecast accuracy to take into account, all of which are positive factors for the business.

The current valuation is closer to 16-17x P/E, and this is enough to mark it an undervaluation. However, in that 26-27x P/E premium, we also have this period to consider - which I do not believe should be considered "valid" in the longer-term framework/valuation context.

So, while a premium may be justified, I'm not putting it at 26-27x P/E. There's too much of 2019-2021 in that, and I have specifically avoided reinvesting in such companies after they drop based on the assumption they will go back up there.

So we'll take a longer-term stance. The 15-20-year average comes closer to 18-20x, which I view as a better estimate. I do not see Bechtle trading as it did post-GFC - the company didn't have a yield (almost) and is much better now than it was at the time.

I would put the company at a maximum P/E of 20x.

Unfortunately, based on a current growth estimate of 5-6% on average for the next few years, that means that the upside is less than 15% per year, even with the company's dividend included. If we were to include the 0.8% shareholder yield we can attribute to net debt paydowns, it might rise above this, but it's still too much of a return issue (with my requirement being 15% or above) for me to go in here. If the company were to drop below €30/share for the native, the return would be good enough, but here it simply isn't.

Because of this, I have no choice but to take a somber and more careful view of Bechtle in my introductory piece. What I want to see is Bechtle at a price where 15% annualized isn't only "just there" but where there even might be a safety margin for it at 19-20x P/E.

That comes to about €30/share or below. Current street targets for the company take a different view, with only a few targets at or below that target of mine. The low-end analyst target for Bechtle at this time is €29/share, with a high of €51/share, coming to an average of about €41/share Despite this upside, only 5 out of 12 analysts have the company at a "Buy", with 6 at either "Hold" or "Underperform", signalling a conviction problem to me. At €33/share compared to the average, there's more than a 24% upside to be had here - yet not even 50% of analysts consider it a buy.

I will come in at a conservative €30/share PT, meaning buying below or at €30/share for the native ticker. This allows an easy 15% or above at 20x P/E, and even a double-digit return at an 18x P/E, which is very close to where the company is trading today. Given its earnings accuracy, its fundamentals, its yields, and its solid base, I consider Bechtle to be worth your attention here.

Closest peers would be CapGemini and Atos or SAP. CapGemini, a French giant I also cover, trades at a lower P/E and has BBB+, as well as a better yield. While the company's growth is lower, I consider CapGemini the far better choice at this time around on a peer basis, which I will illustrate using this 15x P/E upside for the French giant.

CapGemini is, therefore, my first choice in the European IT sector at this time, owing to better fundamentals and valuation. The company is also 4-5x the size of Bechtle in terms of market cap. It comes with other challenges, but I believe these are secondary to valuation here.

Bechtle's ADR is BECTY, a 0.2x ADR, which means that 5 BECTY equals one native share. My target for BECTY, translated and adjusted for size, is $6.76/share.

Therefore, my thesis for the company is as follows.

Thesis

  • Bechtle is a fundamentally solid IT company with an upside at a conservative valuation. We have an upside; we have a relatively conservative valuation in terms of the 20-year average, but unfortunately, it includes exuberant periods of ZIRP, which we need to discount for.
  • That being said, the company's margin, management, debt fundamentals, and results are all positive - and I expect an annualized rate of EPS growth in excess of 5%. The company has none of the risks, such as excessive debt, that weigh other companies down in the same sector.
  • i give the company a conservative PT of €30/share, at which point I would consider the upside attractive enough to invest in.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €30/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2025.
