data.title: Markets Consolidate, But Nervously So, Remaining One Social Media Post Away From Euphoria Or Doom
data.description:
Overview

The postponement of US reciprocal tariffs, the "clarification" that popular consumer products, including those from China, are not subject to the reciprocal tariffs until the levy on semiconductor chips are decided, and a hint from President Trump himself that he is considering a delay in the auto tariffs that were to be effective on May 3 has steadied investor nerves. The dollar is mostly consolidating within the pre-weekend range, and equities have stabilized. In the currency market, sterling and the Australian dollar have shown some independent strength and have extended their gains. The Canadian dollar is threatening the same. Emerging market currencies are mixed, and no clean regional breakdown is evident.

Most Asia Pacific equity markets are extending yesterday's gain. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is up a little more than 1% after advancing 2.70% yesterday. If sustained, it would be the first back-to-back gains since March 18-19. US index futures are firmer. European benchmark 10-year yields are mostly 2-3 basis points firmer. The 10-year Gilt yield is off slightly more than a single basis points and is the best performer among back bond markets today. The 10-year US Treasury yield is practically flat near 4.38%. Gold is consolidating with a firm bias inside yesterday's range, which was inside Friday's range, when the record was set near $3246. May WTI is quiet, mostly between $61.25 and $62.00 today.

USD: The Dollar Index consolidated yesterday, within the pre-weekend range but it still settled lower for the fifth consecutive session. It is trading quietly in a narrow range (~99.50-100.00) inside yesterday's range. The price action looks like nesting before another leg lower. The US threatens that sectoral tariff on semiconductors and pharmaceuticals (and possibly lumber and diary) will still be forthcoming. Yesterday, President Trump said he was considering a delaying the 25% tariffs on autos and parts that were to go into effect on May 3. That and the postponement of the reciprocal tariffs is understood by many as revealing the administration's pain threshold in terms of the dramatic pressure on US stocks and bonds. We suspect that the main check that will arise on US trade policy will not come from the markets or the judiciary, but from Congress, which has the constitutional power to "regulate commerce." It may take a while longer, but the wheels appear to be in motion. Today's April NY Fed manufacturing survey and import/export prices are not typically market-movers. The source of US inflation is not coming from imports. The year-over-year pace may have slowed to 1.4% from 2.0% in March, which would unwind the recent gains. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for unchanged imports on the month.

Euro: The euro stalled yesterday after rising by 3.7% in the previous two session. It is trading inside yesterday's range, which was inside last Friday's. Provided it holds above the $1.1250 area, it looks constructive. A break would initially target the $1.1180 area. Industrial production rose by 1.1% in February % after rising by 0.6% in January (revised from 0.8%). It was well above expectations for a 0.3% rise and matches the largest increase since February 2023. Meanwhile, the slide in the DAX and the US tariffs may have weighed on investor sentiment. April's ZEW survey saw not only the first decline in expectations in three months, but the setback unwound the earlier gains. It fell to -14.0 from 51.6, its lowest since July 2023. The current assessment improved for the fourth consecutive month, and at -81.2 is the best since last August.

CNY: The dollar rose against the offshore yuan for the first time in four sessions yesterday, and simply recouped its pre-weekend loss. It is a bit firmer today, but it remains below last Friday's high (~CNH7.3365). It may continue to consolidate. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rare at CNY7.2096 (CNY7.2110 yesterday) n onshore yuan is practically flat against the dollar this year. That means that it has depreciated against the most of its trading partners. It does not need to offset the US tariff hike with more currency depreciation. How can it? Imagine you are in Beijing. You are watching the US hike tariffs. While the wider change in US foreign policy being implemented by Washington is more disruptive of its alliance, but on trade, China is a key target. Beijing has de-risked from the US. In 2017, the US accounted for about 20% of China's exports. Now around 15%, but that was still $400 bln in 2024. After seemingly announcing prohibitively high tariffs on Chinese goods on April 2, the US indicated that almost 1/4 of its imports from China (consumer electronics) would not face such a tariff after all, and indeed levies collected will returned. This will give producers and importers more time to build inventory before the semiconductor sectoral tariffs are implemented. Beijing's mantra has shifted from "reform and opening up" to "self-reliance and hard work." China orders a halt in Boeing (BA) deliveries today. Estimates suggest Chinese airlines were to take delivery of 10 Boeing 737 Max aircraft. There may be some exceptions granted on a case-by-case basis, depending on what "paperwork and payments" may have been completed before the PRC tariffs were implemented on April 12. Tomorrow, China reports its first estimate of Q1 25 GDP and March details. Beijing says the economy will grow around 5% this year. So, it will.

JPY: Yesterday, the dollar spent the session inside last Friday's range (~JPY142.05-JPY144.65). It is inside yesterday's range today and trading between about JPY142.70 and JPY143.60. The US 10-year yield fell around 11 bps and settled below the pre-weekend low (4.38%). If the nearly 75 bps (from low to high) last week rise in US 10-year yield was exaggerated and extends its pullback, the dollar looks vulnerable. A break of JPY142 could open the door toward JPY140.00-JPY140.50. After a sharp 3.5% drop in core machine orders, a snapback is expected to be the first thing tomorrow. However, the key take-away is that the Japanese economy is slowing considerably this quarter. At an annualized rate, the world's third-largest economy grew an average almost 1.2% in 2024 and that is with a 2.1% contraction in Q1 24. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey puts Q1 25 GDP at 0.3% (annualized) and 0.5% for the next couple of quarters. The swaps market almost 15 bps of tightening discounted, down from 30 bps at the end of March.

GBP: Sterling rose a cent yesterday and tested the $1.32-area, holding slightly below the April 3 high (~$1.3205). It has reached a new high near $1.3240 today, leaving intraday momentum indicators stretched ahead of the North American open. Some attributed it to some short covering against the euro ahead of today's jobs report. Sterling settled above its upper Bollinger Band, which comes in near $1.3200 today. Neither the employment report, nor tomorrow's inflation data, will likely alter views that most likely (90%+ in the swaps market) that the Bank of England will cut rates at its May 8 meeting. All else being equal, the rise of sterling and the decline in oil prices give it scope to reduce rates and support the economy, which practically stagnated in H2 24. Average earnings growth remains more elevated than MPC members prefer even though they softened slightly, and that underscores the gradual pace of the easing cycle. Unemployment remains at 4.4% where it has been now for four months, which is the highest since Q3 21.

CAD: The greenback's session low yesterday was recorded in early European turnover near CAD1.3830. It hardly traded below CAD1.3850 in the local session and rose to session highs around CAD1.3910 after European markets closed and has been capped there today. The US dollar is threatening to extend its slump for a fifth session today. It has not taken out yesterday's low yet, but it looks poised to in the North American session. Canada reports March housing starts and existing home sales, but the focus is on the CPI. A firm reading is expected. The headline rate is expected to rise by around 0.7% which, given the base effect, may mean that the year-over-year rate only ticks up to 2.7% from 2.6%. The underlying core measures are also expected to rise slightly (3.0% from 2.9%). A 0.7% increase in March would put the Q1 annualized pace at 5.7%, but this is partly a payback from the flat reading in Q4. That means that the six-month annualized rate would be around 3.8%, still elevated. The Bank of Canada meets tomorrow, but with next week's election and the firmer core inflation may encourage the standpat stance. It frontloaded cuts last year, and the swaps market has 47 bps of cuts discounted for the remainder of the year, down from about 55 bps at the end of March.

AUD: In the past four sessions, the Australian dollar has rallied nearly 7.25%. It has recovered from a five-year low (~$0.5915) to about $0.6345 yesterday. It is extending its recovery today and approached $0.6380. With one exception, it has not settled above $0.6400 since last December. Momentum indicators are favorable, and Australia made it to US Treasury Secretary Bessent's trade-negotiations short-list, which is also constructive. The futures market has a roughly 30% chance of a half-point cut next month. It was around 65% in the middle of last week. We suspect that the market may still be exaggerating the risk. The minutes from this month's central bank meeting seem to signal a rate cut next month after the election, encouraged by expectations that Q1 CPI (due April 30) will show a moderation in prices, but there did not appear to be a hint that a larger "catch-up" move was being considered.

MXN: After stalling last Thursday and Friday, the dollar took another leg down against the peso yesterday, falling to almost MXN20.02. Recall that last week, the greenback set a two-month high near MXN21.08. It is trapped near yesterday's lows in consolidative turnover (~MXN20.05-MXN20 .11). A break of the MXN20.00 area could spur a re-test of the five-month low recorded on April 3, slightly below MXN19.84. It also held then above the 200-day moving average, which was around MXN19.81. Now it is closer to MXN19.89.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Berkeley: A Luxurious Project Investors Are Betting On
data.description:
Trading at less than 10 times earnings, Berkeley (OTCPK:BKGFY) stock may seem like a no-brainer buy, especially when it's the cheapest UK housebuilder stock amongst the big five. But for all that, a declining earnings outlook over the medium term indicates this could very well be a value trap. That being said, if investors believe in its longer-term project, it may be worth an investment.

Moving Out Costs Berkeley

As a whole, Berkeley's interim results weren't too shabby. Completions were up a healthy 17.8% to 2,103 homes － the company's highest rate in H1 since 2017. Unfortunately, though, this was overshadowed by the relatively stark decline in Average Selling Price [ASP] of 3.8% to £600k. As a consequence, revenue increased by a more modest 7.3% to £1.28B.

Nonetheless, thanks to better economies of scale which were complemented by stabilising build cost inflation, gross margin saw a tick up of 33bps to c.26.5%. This meant that gross profit also rose 8.6% to £339MM. Paired with operating efficiencies in the back office, EBIT margin also realised a lift of 74bps to c.20.2%, resulting in EBIT rising 11.4% to £258MM.

Net finance income was £5MM better than last year, at £10MM. But due to St Edward's shift to lower-value developments in the South East from London this year, JV income went from £54MM to a meagre £7MM this year. Thus, PAT ended up being 7.5% lower at £196MM. This translated to basic EPS dropping 5.8% to 186.8p, as the fall was offset slightly by share buybacks.

Still, management reiterated its guidance over the medium term. It continues to expect PBT of £975MM for FY25 (£525MM) and FY26 (£450MM), with an EBIT margin of 17.5-19.5%. However, it did shift expectations for net cash to a range of £250-400MM by FY26 from a previous £400MM, as they anticipate more cash being used to buy more land, as part of the Berkeley 2035 strategy.

On that point, it's Berkeley's 2035 strategy that is one of the reasons behind why both the team and analysts alike are projecting for Berkeley's top and bottom lines to fall through to FY27. It comes down to two standout reasons － a slowdown in prevailing sales rates, and higher investments in land to scale its Build to Rent [BTR] platform.

Top and Bottom Lines Head South

This is why although peers like Bellway (OTCPK:BLWYF) and Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODF), have seen their order books and sales rates pick up, Berkeley's hasn't. This is because Berkeley's product mix is centred around upscale properties, especially in flats in London and the South-East, where housing starts have come in significantly below targets, and even more so over the past year.

In fact, house prices in the UK has been a story of two tales over the past year. Data from Rightmove (OTCPK:RTMVF) shows quite a disparity in house price appreciation between the North and South － an indicator of supply/demand dynamics. Most prominently, areas like London and the South, where Berkeley builds most of its properties, have experienced much lower increases.

Not only that, it's also worth noting that the Surrey-based builder predominantly builds flats/apartment complexes as opposed to detached or terraced homes. As such, it hasn't been able to realise the appreciation of its properties, as flats have had the lowest price growth over the past year, according to Nationwide data.

Additionally, Berkeley is allocating quite a number of its builds to its BTR portfolio. As a result, the builder will see a reduction in revenue from completion sales, as the revenue is deferred to rental income instead. The idea here is that revenue will accrue over time to 'out-earn' what it would've otherwise gained from just selling the properties themselves.

Moreover, Berkeley can't just build its way to higher revenue either. Given its position in the South-East, where many historical sights are located, getting approval to build new homes is an even longer and more tedious process. The new Labour government's efforts to trim the red tape down are positive, but they may take a while before Berkeley can actualise them into results.

An Expensive Investment?

Having said that, Berkeley still stands to gain over the long term from the Labour government's brownfield-first approach. Officials have proposed changes to the National Planning Policy Framework [NPPF] to prioritise brownfield development, with the policies aiming to transform neglected urban land such as abandoned industrial sites into housing.

This is particularly good for Berkeley because it tends to build many of its apartment complexes on regenerated brownfield land, which is extremely common in London and the South-East. As a matter of fact, c.92.0% of the homes it built in H1 were built on brownfield land. Given the aforementioned regulatory stumbling blocks, these policies are a much-needed bulldozer.

But where long-term investors may find Berkeley's full potential is in its BTR project, with the first deliveries scheduled for FY27. While the board has yet to disclose how much in sales the portfolio could fetch, our calculations indicate anywhere between £80-120MM/yr when all 4,000 units are ready to rent out in FY30, with the scale to build even more thereafter.

Be that as it may, there are also numerous headwinds that could undermine the earnings outlook. Amongst these include the higher Residential Property Developer Tax [RPDT], on top of the 25.0% corporation tax, plus discussions of a Building Safety Levy [BSL], and higher build cost inflation from mandatory wage hikes. These all could constrain Berkeley’s ability to maintain its margins.

But as we don't cover the stock, we refrain from issuing an official rating. That said, our model suggests downside of c.3.0%, with a forward dividend yield of c.6.4%. This is derived by comparing Berkeley's key ratios to their historical and sector(s) averages, before averaging their resulting fair values altogether. Although, we stress that this is contingent on consensus estimates.

Price Model Berkeley Sector AVG 5Y AVG Fair Value
P/B (20% wt) 1.1 1.0 1.6 4,515p
EV/EBITDA (20% wt) 6.4 10.0 8.7 5,489p
P/E (20% wt) 10.2 15.3 11.7 4,972p
FP/E (20% wt) 10.91 16.7 12.8 5,085p
PEG (20% wt) -2.6 1.5 2.1 -2,612p
Fair Value 3,500p

***As a reminder to our readers, Berkeley's primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange (LSE:BKG). Trading this stock on OTCMKTS may present liquidity risks resulting in higher bid-ask spreads. For optimal execution and more accurate price discovery, we recommend assessing the stock on the LSE instead, where possible.

Explaining Our Approach and Model

When it comes to our valuation model, we employ a combination of multiples approach, rather than the infamous Discounted Cash Flow [DCF] model, which many investment banks utilise. To explain, our methodology centres on utilising multiple valuation ratios:

  • P/B (20% wt);
  • EV/EBITDA (20% wt);
  • P/E (20% wt);
  • Forward P/E (20% wt);
  • (Forward) PEG (20% wt);

While we normally prioritise forward earnings metrics because stock prices predominantly reflect future earnings expectations, the lower EPS forecasts have forced us to incorporate some intrinsic value multiples. We've also included trailing ratios in order to provide analytical balance. This is to mitigate the effects of volatility or potential M&A activity.

We believe this approach delivers greater accuracy than a DCF, for the reason that it can account for critical factors such as stock buybacks more accurately. Furthermore, DCFs typically produce significantly higher standard deviations, as minor adjustments to the Weighted Average Cost of Capital [WACC] and/or discount rates can dramatically alter price targets.

In addition to that, DCF models often contain "black box" elements with inadequate explanations regarding the discount rate, WACC determinations, and more crucially, terminal value calculations from perpetual growth rates. On the other hand, our combination of multiples model is more transparent, and more digestible for investors.

Multiples Appendix

  1. P/B (20% wt)
    • As of H1'25 results.
    • Book value is calculated from total equity, divided by basic shares outstanding as of latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
  2. EV/EBITDA (20% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1'25 results.
    • Market cap data for EV is calculated from multiplying shares outstanding as of latest RNS with the latest share price.
    • EBITDA is calculated from: EBIT + Depreciation.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes positive EBITDA firms.
  3. P/E (20% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1'25 results.
    • EPS is calculated from profit after tax, divided by basic shares outstanding as of latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes money-making firms.
  4. FP/E (20% wt)
    • Usually calculated from using weighted forward projections if current period falls between fiscal years.
    • For example, if the current TTM is to H1'25, the calculation will be: (FY25 projected EPS - H1'25 EPS) + (FY26 projected EPS / 100 x historical weight of H1 EPS on FY EPS).
    • EPS is calculated from FTM profit after tax, divided by projected basic shares outstanding.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by S&P Global Market Intelligence.
  5. FPEG (20% wt)
    • First, CAGR is calculated as: (FY27 EPS / TTM EPS) ^ (1 / 2.5 yrs) - 1.
    • Then, FPEG is calculated as: (Current share price / TTM EPS) / CAGR.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by S&P Global Market Intelligence.
